_disclaimer: ALL ITEMS IN THIS FILE ARE SYNTHETIC PLACEHOLDERS, hand-authored to exercise the LSAT Item contract. They are NOT real LSAT items and must NOT be used as study material. Real items must be sourced from official LSAC PrepTests with redistribution licensing (D-SR11). Every item carries SyntheticFlag=SYNTHETIC.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] After the town installed brighter streetlights, nighttime traffic accidents fell. Clearly, the brighter lights made the roads safer.
items.Stem: Which one of the following, if true, most weakens the argument?
items.ChoiceA: The brighter streetlights cost more than the town expected.
items.ChoiceB: Daytime accident rates were unchanged over the period.
items.ChoiceC: Residents said they felt safer after the lights were installed.
items.ChoiceD: A few drivers complained that the new lights were too bright.
items.ChoiceE: The town also lowered the nighttime speed limit at the same time.
items.WhyWrongA: Cost has no bearing on whether the lights caused fewer accidents.
items.WhyWrongB: Unchanged daytime rates are neutral to the nighttime claim.
items.WhyWrongC: Feeling safer does not show the lights caused the decline.
items.WhyWrongD: A few complaints do not address the causal claim.
items.WhyWrongE: CORRECT. A simultaneous speed-limit cut is an alternative cause of the accident drop.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] A vitamin maker claims its supplement boosts immunity, noting that people who take it report fewer colds than people who do not.
items.Stem: Which one of the following, if true, most undermines the claim?
items.ChoiceA: The supplement is sold in most major pharmacies.
items.ChoiceB: Colds are generally more frequent during winter months.
items.ChoiceC: People who take the supplement also tend to exercise more and eat better.
items.ChoiceD: Some people who take the supplement still catch colds.
items.ChoiceE: The supplement contains both vitamin C and zinc.
items.WhyWrongB: Seasonality affects both groups and does not explain the difference.
items.WhyWrongC: CORRECT. Healthier habits among users is an alternative explanation for the fewer colds.
items.WhyWrongD: 'Fewer colds' is consistent with some users still getting colds.
items.WhyWrongE: Ingredients do not by themselves undermine the comparison.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] A historian argues that the empire fell because of economic decline, pointing to shrinking trade records in its final century.
items.Stem: Which one of the following, if true, most weakens the historian's argument?
items.ChoiceA: Economic decline can result from a wide variety of causes.
items.ChoiceB: The empire is known today for its impressive architecture.
items.ChoiceC: Trade was an important part of the empire's economy.
items.ChoiceD: The historian has studied the empire for several decades.
items.ChoiceE: Many trade records from that century were later destroyed, so surviving records understate actual trade.
items.WhyWrongA: General causal variety does not address this argument's evidence.
items.WhyWrongB: Architecture is irrelevant to the economic-decline claim.
items.WhyWrongC: That trade mattered is background that, if anything, supports the argument.
items.WhyWrongD: The historian's experience is an appeal, not evidence about trade.
items.WhyWrongE: CORRECT. If records were lost, shrinking records may not reflect shrinking trade — the key evidence is undercut.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] The town's new curfew for teenagers reduced crime: since the curfew took effect, the number of crimes committed by teenagers has fallen.
items.Stem: Which one of the following, if true, most weakens the argument?
items.ChoiceA: Crime committed by adults in the town was essentially unchanged.
items.ChoiceB: The curfew was widely publicized before it took effect.
items.ChoiceC: A majority of residents said they supported the curfew.
items.ChoiceD: During the same period, the town's teenage population dropped sharply as many families moved away.
items.ChoiceE: Teenage crime had been rising in the years just before the curfew.
items.WhyWrongA: Unchanged adult crime is neutral toward the teen-curfew claim.
items.WhyWrongB: Publicity does not undercut the causal claim.
items.WhyWrongC: Support for the curfew says nothing about its effect.
items.WhyWrongD: CORRECT. If there were far fewer teenagers, teen crime could fall for that reason alone, not because of the curfew.
items.WhyWrongE: A prior rise, now reversed, tends to support the curfew's effectiveness.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Eating dark chocolate improves heart health: a study found that people who eat dark chocolate regularly suffer fewer heart attacks than those who do not.
items.Stem: Which one of the following, if true, most weakens the argument?
items.ChoiceA: Heart attacks are among the leading causes of death worldwide.
items.ChoiceB: Dark chocolate contains naturally occurring compounds called flavonoids.
items.ChoiceC: Dark chocolate is generally more expensive than milk chocolate.
items.ChoiceD: The study followed its participants for more than ten years.
items.ChoiceE: People who regularly eat dark chocolate also tend to exercise more and eat more vegetables.
items.WhyWrongA: The prevalence of heart attacks does not bear on the causal claim.
items.WhyWrongB: A plausible mechanism strengthens rather than weakens.
items.WhyWrongC: Price is irrelevant to heart health.
items.WhyWrongD: A long follow-up makes the study more credible, not less.
items.WhyWrongE: CORRECT. A healthier overall lifestyle among chocolate eaters could explain their fewer heart attacks, undercutting the chocolate-causes-health claim.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] The company's advertising campaign must have caused its sales increase, because sales rose 15% in the month after the campaign launched.
items.Stem: Which one of the following, if true, most weakens the argument?
items.ChoiceA: The company's sales are usually tallied at the end of each month.
items.ChoiceB: The company's competitors ran no advertising campaigns during that month.
items.ChoiceC: The campaign was designed by an award-winning advertising agency.
items.ChoiceD: The advertising campaign appeared on both television and online.
items.ChoiceE: The company cut the prices of its products by 20% during that same month.
items.WhyWrongA: When sales are tallied is irrelevant.
items.WhyWrongB: Idle competitors, if anything, support attributing the rise to the campaign.
items.WhyWrongC: The agency's reputation does not weaken the claim.
items.WhyWrongD: The channels used do not undercut the causal claim.
items.WhyWrongE: CORRECT. A simultaneous 20% price cut is a strong alternative explanation for the sales jump.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] The region's lakes are growing more acidic because of emissions from the new factory: the lakes' acidity rose after the factory opened.
items.Stem: Which one of the following, if true, most weakens the argument?
items.ChoiceA: Some other regions that also have factories have acidic lakes.
items.ChoiceB: The factory employs several hundred workers who live locally.
items.ChoiceC: Lakes that are more acidic tend to support fewer species of fish.
items.ChoiceD: The factory releases several kinds of airborne pollutants into the area.
items.ChoiceE: The lakes' acidity had been rising steadily for several decades before the factory opened.
items.WhyWrongA: Other regions' lakes do not explain away this factory's suspected role.
items.WhyWrongB: Local employment does not bear on the acidity's cause.
items.WhyWrongC: Effects of acidity on fish are irrelevant to its cause.
items.WhyWrongD: That the factory emits pollutants supports, not weakens, the claim.
items.WhyWrongE: CORRECT. A long-standing upward trend predating the factory shows the acidity has another cause, undercutting the factory explanation.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Since violent video games cause aggression, banning them would reduce violent crime among young people.
items.Stem: Which one of the following, if true, most weakens the argument?
items.ChoiceA: Many of the most popular video games contain some violent content.
items.ChoiceB: Overall video game sales have risen steadily over the past decade.
items.ChoiceC: In countries that ban violent video games, youth violent-crime rates are no lower than in comparable countries that permit them.
items.ChoiceD: Some parents closely monitor and restrict which video games their children play.
items.ChoiceE: Playing competitive sports can also raise aggression in some people.
items.WhyWrongA: The popularity of violent games does not bear on whether banning them reduces crime.
items.WhyWrongB: Rising sales say nothing about the effect of a ban on crime.
items.WhyWrongC: CORRECT. If banning the games yields no lower youth crime, the proposed policy would not achieve its aim, directly undercutting the argument.
items.WhyWrongD: Parental monitoring is beside the point.
items.WhyWrongE: That sports also raise aggression does not address the ban's effect on crime.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] The library's summer reading program boosts children's literacy: children who complete the program read better in the fall than children who do not.
items.Stem: Which one of the following, if true, most weakens the argument?
items.ChoiceA: Children who complete the program spend more time reading over the summer than other children do.
items.ChoiceB: Children who are already strong readers are far more likely to sign up for and complete the program.
items.ChoiceC: The summer reading program has been offered every year for a decade.
items.ChoiceD: The program offers small prizes to children who finish it.
items.ChoiceE: The fall reading tests are administered by specially trained staff.
items.WhyWrongA: This supports the program's role rather than weakening it.
items.WhyWrongB: CORRECT. If strong readers self-select into completing the program, their better fall scores may reflect prior ability, not the program's effect.
items.WhyWrongC: The program's longevity is irrelevant to its effect.
items.WhyWrongD: Prizes do not undercut the literacy claim.
items.WhyWrongE: Who administers the tests does not bear on the causal claim.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] The mayor claims her jobs program created 2,000 new jobs, noting that citywide employment rose by 2,000 during the year the program ran.
items.Stem: Which one of the following, if true, most weakens the mayor's claim?
items.ChoiceA: The jobs program turned out to be more expensive than initially expected.
items.ChoiceB: The city's employment figures are compiled by an independent agency.
items.ChoiceC: During that year, a large factory unrelated to the program opened in the city and hired 2,500 workers.
items.ChoiceD: The jobs program was funded partly by a grant from the state government.
items.ChoiceE: The mayor had promised to create jobs during her election campaign.
items.WhyWrongA: Cost overruns do not address how many jobs were created.
items.WhyWrongB: An independent tally, if anything, lends credibility to the figures.
items.WhyWrongC: CORRECT. If an unrelated factory added 2,500 jobs, the citywide rise of 2,000 could be explained without the program (indeed net program jobs could be negative).
items.WhyWrongD: The funding source does not bear on how many jobs the program created.
items.WhyWrongE: A campaign promise is irrelevant to the program's actual effect.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] The clinic's new painkiller is more effective than the old one: patients who received the new drug reported less pain than those who received the old drug.
items.Stem: Which one of the following, if true, most weakens the argument?
items.ChoiceA: Pain levels were recorded using a standard self-report scale.
items.ChoiceB: The new painkiller is manufactured by a well-known pharmaceutical company.
items.ChoiceC: The patients given the new drug had, on average, considerably milder injuries than those given the old drug.
items.ChoiceD: Both drugs were administered to patients by the same nurses.
items.ChoiceE: The new painkiller costs more per dose than the old one.
items.WhyWrongA: A standard measurement scale, if anything, supports the comparison.
items.WhyWrongB: The manufacturer's fame does not bear on effectiveness.
items.WhyWrongC: CORRECT. If new-drug patients started with milder injuries, their lower reported pain may reflect the injuries, not the drug's superiority.
items.WhyWrongD: Same nurses is a control that does not weaken the claim.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Because the ferry service was expanded, tourism to the island grew: after the ferry added more daily crossings, the number of tourists rose sharply.
items.Stem: Which one of the following, if true, most weakens the argument?
items.ChoiceA: Ferry tickets cost the same after the expansion as they did before.
items.ChoiceB: The same year the ferry expanded, the island opened a large new resort that drew many visitors.
items.ChoiceC: Tourism to nearby islands remained flat during the same year.
items.ChoiceD: The expanded ferry service uses newer, faster boats than before.
items.ChoiceE: The island is widely known for its scenic beaches.
items.WhyWrongA: Unchanged fares are irrelevant to the cause of the increase.
items.WhyWrongB: CORRECT. A major new resort opening the same year is an alternative cause for the surge in tourists.
items.WhyWrongC: Flat tourism nearby rules out a regional trend, supporting the ferry explanation.
items.WhyWrongD: Faster boats do not undercut the causal claim.
items.WhyWrongE: The island's scenery is background, not a competing cause tied to that year.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Paleontologist: The mass extinction at the close of the era falls in the rock record at exactly the level of enormous volcanic eruptions. The eruptions must therefore have caused the extinction.
items.Stem: Which one of the following, if true, most weakens the paleontologist's argument?
items.ChoiceA: The eruptions released immense quantities of ash and toxic gas into the atmosphere.
items.ChoiceB: A few hardy species survived the extinction and have descendants that are alive today.
items.ChoiceC: The rock layers that record the eruptions are notoriously hard to date with precision.
items.ChoiceD: Eruptions of comparable scale in several earlier eras were followed by no mass extinction whatsoever.
items.ChoiceE: The extinction struck ocean-dwelling and land-dwelling species in roughly equal measure.
items.WhyWrongA: This supports the causal story rather than weakening it.
items.WhyWrongB: That some species survived does not bear on what killed the many that did not.
items.WhyWrongC: Imprecise dating loosens the coincidence only slightly and leaves the causal claim largely standing.
items.WhyWrongD: CORRECT. If equally large eruptions elsewhere caused no extinction, eruptions alone are not sufficient, undercutting the claim that they caused this one.
items.WhyWrongE: The breadth of the extinction says nothing about its cause.
